questions.question: What is the purpose of the base-to-final turn in a traffic pattern?
questions.correct_answer: To align the aircraft with the extended runway centerline for the final approach
distractors: To reduce airspeed before landing
To check for traffic on the runway
To configure the aircraft for a go-around
questions.explanation: The base-to-final turn aligns the aircraft with the runway. Pilots should avoid overshooting this turn, especially at low altitude, as steep turns close to the ground are dangerous.

questions.question: What are the elements of a stabilized approach?
questions.correct_answer: Proper airspeed, descent rate, flight path, and landing configuration established before a predetermined point
distractors: Maintaining a constant engine RPM throughout final
Flying the approach without any power changes
Keeping the wings level at all times during final
questions.explanation: A stabilized approach means the aircraft is at the proper speed, descent rate, configuration (flaps/gear), and on the correct glidepath well before the runway threshold. An unstabilized approach warrants a go-around.

questions.question: In the wing-low (sideslip) method of crosswind landing, how is the aircraft aligned with the runway?
questions.correct_answer: Bank into the wind with opposite rudder to keep the nose aligned with the runway centerline
distractors: Use rudder only into the wind with wings level
Bank away from the wind with same-direction rudder
Apply forward slip with full flaps
questions.explanation: The wing-low method uses aileron to bank into the wind (correcting drift) and opposite rudder to prevent turning and keep the longitudinal axis aligned with the runway.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the flare (roundout) during landing?
questions.correct_answer: To transition from the descent to a landing attitude, reducing the descent rate for a gentle touchdown
distractors: To increase airspeed before touchdown
To level the wings for a straight rollout
To activate ground effect for a shorter landing
questions.explanation: The flare gradually changes the flight path from a descent to a level attitude, dissipating energy and reducing sink rate so the aircraft touches down gently on the main gear.

questions.question: What is the first action a pilot should take when initiating a go-around?
distractors: Retract the flaps fully
Raise the landing gear immediately
Pull back sharply to climb
questions.explanation: The first action is to apply full power to arrest the descent and begin climbing. Then adjust pitch for climb attitude, reduce flaps incrementally (not all at once to avoid sink), and retract gear as appropriate.

questions.question: What approach speed is typically used for a short-field landing?
questions.correct_answer: 1.3 times the stall speed in landing configuration (1.3 VSO), or the POH recommended speed
distractors: Normal approach speed plus 10 knots
Best glide speed
1.5 times the stall speed
questions.explanation: Short-field approaches use the minimum safe speed, typically 1.3 VSO (or as specified in the POH), to touch down at the lowest possible speed and minimize rollout distance.

questions.question: What is the key technique difference in a soft-field landing versus a normal landing?
questions.correct_answer: Touch down at the slowest possible speed in a nose-high attitude and keep back pressure to hold weight off the nosewheel
distractors: Touch down faster than normal to prevent getting stuck
Land nose-first to distribute weight forward
Apply brakes immediately upon touchdown
questions.explanation: On soft fields, the goal is to touch down as slowly as possible in a full-stall or near-stall attitude and hold the nosewheel off to prevent it from digging into the soft surface.

questions.question: What is the difference between a forward slip and a sideslip?
questions.correct_answer: A forward slip is used to lose altitude without gaining airspeed; a sideslip corrects for crosswind drift
distractors: There is no difference; they are the same maneuver
A forward slip uses only rudder; a sideslip uses only ailerons
A forward slip is performed at high altitude; a sideslip only near the ground
questions.explanation: In a forward slip, the aircraft's nose is pointed away from the flight path to create drag and steepen descent. In a sideslip, the aircraft tracks straight along the ground while banked into a crosswind.
